answers.intake_q1: Can you describe the chest pain? For example, is it sharp, dull, pressure-like, or burning?
answers.intake_q2: When did the chest pain start?
answers.intake_q3: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or pain radiating to your arm or jaw?
answers.intake_q4: Where exactly is the chest pain located, and does it move anywhere else?
answers.intake_a4: next to sternum on the right and it doesn't move anywhere else
answers.intake_q5: Any recent trauma, heavy lifting, or coughing that might have triggered the chest pain?
answers.intake_q6: Any swelling, redness, or tenderness over the area where you feel the pain?
answers.intake_q7: Any history of heart disease, blood clots, or lung problems?
answers.intake_q8: Any difficulty breathing with exertion or at rest since the pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the chest pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_q10: Any fever, chills, or recent infections?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with sharp chest pain located next to the sternum on the right side, which has been present for one week. The pain does not radiate and is associated with tenderness over the area. The pain began after heavy lifting. She denies shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, pain radiating to the arm or jaw, fever, chills, recent infections, or history of heart disease, blood clots, or lung problems. She has not tried any treatments or medications for the pain. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal chest pain likely due to strain or injury from heavy lifting, possibly costochondritis given the localized tenderness and sharp pain near the sternum. 
Differential: 
1. Costochondritis or musculoskeletal chest wall strain 
2. Rib fracture or contusion from heavy lifting 
3. Precordial catch syndrome 
4. Less likely cardiac or pulmonary causes given absence of associated symptoms 

P: 
- First-line medication: 
 Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6–8 hours as needed for pain, for 7 days. Avoid if history of gastrointestinal ulcers or bleeding. Dosing details were not found in retrieved evidence; prescriber should verify. 

- Alternatives: 
 - Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain, for 7 days; avoid in severe liver disease. 
 - Naproxen 250–500 mg orally twice daily as needed for pain, for 7 days; avoid in gastrointestinal ulcers or bleeding. 

- Medication safety considerations: 
 No known medication allergies. Not pregnant. No renal or liver disease reported. Use NSAIDs cautiously if gastrointestinal risk factors develop. 

- Return precautions: 
 Seek urgent care if chest pain worsens, becomes constant, radiates to arm or jaw, or is associated with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or syncope. 

- Testing: 
 If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing/workup as indicated.

evidence.title: Troponin Test: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet: mild or intense, and they can come and go over several hours. They include: Chest pain, heaviness, or discomfort in the center or left side of the chest Pain or discomfort in one or both arms, your back, shoulders, neck, jaw, or above your belly button Trouble breathing when resting or doing light physical activity Nausea and vomiting Feeling unusually tired…

evidence.title: Heart Attack | Myocardial Infarction | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: he chest. It usually lasts more than a few minutes. It may go away and come back. It can feel like pressure, squeezing, fullness, or pain . It also can feel like heartburn or indigestion. Shortness of breath. Sometimes this is your only symptom. You may get it before or during the chest discomfort. It can happen when you are resting or doing a little bit of…

evidence.title: Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) - NHS
evidence.snippet: us trigger can happen at any age, but often starts for the first time in children and young adults – many people have their first symptoms between 25 and 40 years old You may get no other symptoms, but sometimes people also: have chest pain or discomfort feel weak, breathless, lightheaded or dizzy feel tired Non-urgent advice: See a GP if: you keep getting a…

evidence.title: Heart attack - NHS
evidence.snippet: ack Symptoms of a heart attack can include: chest pain – a feeling of pressure, heaviness, tightness or squeezing across your chest pain in other parts of the body – it can feel as if the pain is spreading from your chest to your arms (usually the left arm, but it can affect both arms), jaw, neck, back and tummy feeling lightheaded or dizzy sweating shortnes…

evidence.title: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome - NHS
evidence.snippet: and can range from every day to a few times a year or less.
